title: The young weightlifter with a 'second wind'
blurb: Cramps and dark urine minutes into hard exercise, then relief if he pushes through — muscle that can't tap its own glycogen.

patient.complaint: Severe muscle cramps and cola-colored urine after intense exercise.

opening: 22-year-old man reports that within minutes of intense exertion his muscles cramp painfully and he has to stop — but if he rests briefly and resumes gently, it eases (a 'second wind'). Twice he has passed cola-colored urine after lifting weights, once with a CK of 25,000. At rest he is completely well. The forearm ischemic exercise test shows a FLAT lactate but a normal ammonia rise. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Lifelong exercise intolerance with cramps on sprinting/lifting; better with sustained mild activity after a 'second wind.' Two episodes of myoglobinuria. No weakness at rest. No family history known (autosomal recessive).
exam: Well-built, normal strength and tone at rest. No fixed weakness, no fasciculations. Cardiac and respiratory exams normal. No hepatomegaly (this is a MUSCLE glycogenosis).

prompts.diagnosis: What is the diagnosis (name + GSD number)?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why the flat lactate on ischemic exercise, and why the 'second wind'?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the first step of glycogen breakdown that fails in muscle here.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — glycogen phosphorylase (the muscle isoform, myophosphorylase). Muscle can't liberate glucose-1-P from its glycogen during burst exercise, so anaerobic glycolysis can't ramp up → flat lactate, energy crisis, cramps, rhabdomyolysis. The 'second wind' comes when blood glucose and fatty acids take over as fuel.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The block is at the FIRST step of glycogen breakdown (glycogen → glucose-1-phosphate).
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: Glycogen phos. (Glycogen → G1P). McArdle = the MUSCLE isoform (PYGM); the liver isoform is spared, so fasting glucose is normal.

mcqs.stem: Why is the lactate rise absent on ischemic forearm exercise?
choices: Muscle can't mobilize its glycogen to feed glycolysis, so no anaerobic lactate is produced
Lactate is rapidly cleared by the liver
mcqs.explanation: The test stresses anaerobic glycolysis. Without myophosphorylase, glycogen can't enter glycolysis, so lactate stays flat while ammonia (from the purine nucleotide cycle, AMP deaminase) still rises normally — confirming effort was made.

choices: After a few minutes of rest/lower effort, blood-borne glucose and free fatty acids fuel the muscle, relieving the early cramping
mcqs.explanation: Early exercise relies on muscle glycogen (unavailable here). After ~7-10 minutes, hepatic glucose and mobilized fatty acids reach the muscle and sustain activity — the hallmark second wind of McArdle disease.

mcqs.stem: How does McArdle differ from von Gierke (GSD I)?
choices: McArdle is a MUSCLE glycogenosis with normal fasting glucose; von Gierke is a LIVER glycogenosis (G6Pase) with fasting hypoglycemia
mcqs.explanation: McArdle = PYGM (muscle), so glucose homeostasis is intact and the picture is exercise intolerance/rhabdo. Von Gierke = G6Pase (liver), so fasting hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, and hepatomegaly dominate.
